title: Memory and Learning
content:
Memory is the process by which information is encoded, stored, and retrieved. Without memory there is no learning, no identity, and no continuity of experience. Psychologists distinguish several memory systems.

Sensory memory holds a brief snapshot of sensory input — iconic memory (visual, ~0.5 s) and echoic memory (auditory, ~3–4 s). It feeds into working memory (short-term memory), which holds about 7 ± 2 items for ~20–30 seconds. George Miller's 1956 paper 'The Magical Number Seven' established this limit.

Long-term memory is vast and relatively permanent. It divides into:

* Explicit (declarative) memory — conscious recall of facts and events
* Episodic memory — personal experiences with time and place (your first day at school)
* Semantic memory — general knowledge and facts (Paris is the capital of France)
* Implicit (non-declarative) memory — unconscious skills and habits (riding a bike)
* Procedural memory — motor skills performed automatically
* Priming — exposure to one stimulus influences response to another

The hippocampus (in the medial temporal lobe) is critical for forming new explicit memories. Patient H.M., who had his hippocampus removed in 1953, could no longer form new long-term memories — a landmark case in memory research.

Learning involves relatively permanent changes in behaviour or knowledge due to experience. Classical conditioning (Pavlov): a neutral stimulus becomes associated with a meaningful one. Operant conditioning (Skinner): behaviour is shaped by its consequences (reinforcement/punishment).

* Encoding failure is the most common cause of 'forgetting' — information never consolidated
* The spacing effect: distributed practice (spaced repetition) is far superior to massed practice (cramming)
* Retrieval cues: context, mood, and state can trigger memory recall
* Reconsolidation: retrieved memories are briefly malleable before being re-stored
* Flashbulb memories feel vivid but are not necessarily more accurate than normal memories
depths:
The multi-store model (Atkinson & Shiffrin, 1968) depicts memory as three sequential stores: sensory → short-term → long-term, with rehearsal as the key mechanism for transfer. However, this was challenged by Baddeley & Hitch's working memory model (1974), which replaced the single short-term store with a multi-component system.

Working memory components: the central executive (controls attention), the phonological loop (handles verbal/auditory information), the visuospatial sketchpad (handles visual and spatial information), and the episodic buffer (integrates information across systems and with long-term memory).

Forgetting theories: Decay theory — traces fade with time. Interference theory — other memories disrupt recall (proactive: old interferes with new; retroactive: new interferes with old). Motivated forgetting — repression of emotionally threatening memories (Freud). Encoding specificity principle — recall is best when retrieval context matches encoding context.

* The 'generation effect': actively producing information (testing yourself) beats passive review
* Elaborative encoding — connecting new information to existing knowledge — improves retention
* Loftus & Palmer (1974): leading questions alter eyewitness testimony — memory is reconstructive
* Tip-of-the-tongue phenomenon: partial recall where you feel you know something but can't access it
* Sleep consolidates memories — the hippocampus replays events during slow-wave sleep
questions: How many items can working memory typically hold?
George Miller's research established ~7 ± 2 items as the capacity of working memory.
Which brain region is critical for forming new explicit memories?
Amygdala
Cerebellum
Hippocampus
Frontal lobe
The hippocampus is essential for consolidating new explicit (declarative) memories.
What is episodic memory?
General factual knowledge
Unconscious motor skills
Personal experiences linked to time and place
Conditioned reflexes
Episodic memory stores autobiographical events with their context.
The spacing effect refers to what?
Studying right before an exam is best
Distributed practice over time improves retention
Short bursts of study are always effective
Repetition within one session is optimal
Spaced repetition — spreading practice over time — leads to far better long-term retention than cramming.
Classical conditioning was pioneered by?
B.F. Skinner
Ivan Pavlov
William James
George Miller
Pavlov discovered classical conditioning through his experiments with dogs and salivation.
flashcards: Short-term store holding ~7 ± 2 items for ~20–30 seconds; manages active thought.
Brain structure essential for forming new long-term explicit memories.
Memory for personal events tied to a specific time and place.
Memory for general knowledge and facts (independent of personal experience).
Neutral stimulus becomes associated with a meaningful stimulus (Pavlov).
Distributed practice over time produces better retention than massed practice.
